title: Post-Delivery High-Value Goods Dispute Pattern with Extended Usage Window
narrative: Cardholder purchases premium electronics or luxury items ($800-$2,500 range) using their legitimate card, receives goods, uses them for 45-90 days, then files chargeback claiming unauthorized transaction. Pattern concentrates in ProductCD 'W' (luxury/electronics). Distinct signature: TransactionAmt consistently above $1,200, delivery confirmation exists in merchant records, dispute filed 6-12 weeks post-purchase (just before chargeback window closes at 120 days). Account shows no fraud velocity signals pre-purchase, card1_txn_count_1day equals 1-2, normal AVS/CVV match. R_emaildomain matches billing email. Cardholder's card1 shows 8-15 prior successful transactions over 6+ months with same merchant category. Time-to-dispute correlates with product depreciation cycleâ€”laptops at 60 days, cameras at 75 days. No addr1 mismatch, dist1 under 50km. Purchase during business hours (txn_hour 9-17), is_night false.
indicators: Chargeback filed 45-90 days post-transaction, approaching 120-day dispute deadline
card1_txn_count_180day shows 8-15 prior transactions, no prior disputes
Perfect AVS and CVV match, R_emaildomain matches P_emaildomain
dist1 under 50km indicating local delivery, addr1 matches cardholder records
card1_amt_zscore between -0.3 and +0.8 (within normal spending pattern)
summary: A legitimate cardholder with 8-15 prior successful transactions purchases high-value electronics ($1,200-$2,500), receives delivery to their verified address with perfect card verification, then files a chargeback 45-90 days later claiming the transaction was unauthorized. The timing consistently aligns with product depreciation cycles—disputes appear around 60 days for laptops, 75 days for cameras—just before the 120-day chargeback deadline.
reasoning: This is friendly fraud where the customer strategically exploits chargeback protections to get a refund after legitimately purchasing and using expensive items. By waiting until the product has depreciated and timing the dispute near the deadline, they retain the used goods while recovering their money, effectively stealing from the merchant under false pretenses of unauthorized charges.
indicator_explanations: Purchase falls in sweet spot for expensive items worth disputing later
Dispute timing suggests buyer used product then claimed fraud near deadline
Cardholder has established transaction history with clean record until now
All verification passed showing legitimate cardholder made the original purchase
Delivery address matches cardholder location eliminating shipping mule scenario
Purchase amount fits cardholder's normal buying behavior without red flags
Transaction made during typical shopping hours suggesting deliberate legitimate purchase
